examples.title: Insertion in Separate Chaining Hash Table
examples.description: Demonstrates how to insert a key-value pair into a hash table using separate chaining for collision resolution
examples.code:
STRUCTURE Node
  key
  value
  next
END STRUCTURE

FUNCTION insertChaining(table, key, value)
  index = hash(key)
  newNode = createNode(key, value)
  
  IF table[index] == NULL THEN
    table[index] = newNode
  ELSE
    newNode.next = table[index]
    table[index] = newNode
  END IF
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION searchChaining(table, key)
  index = hash(key)
  current = table[index]
  
  WHILE current != NULL DO
    IF current.key == key THEN
      RETURN current.value
    END IF
    current = current.next
  END WHILE
  
  RETURN NOT_FOUND
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Insertion adds new node at the beginning of the linked list (O(1)). Search traverses the chain at computed index looking for the key. Average case O(1+α), worst case O(n).
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing n elements plus O(n) overhead for pointers

examples.title: Linear Probing Hash Table Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation of hash table with linear probing for collision resolution
examples.explanation: Linear probing uses probe sequence (h(k) + i) mod m. Insert finds first empty/tombstone slot. Search stops at None but continues past tombstones. Tombstones mark deleted positions to preserve probe sequences.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) for table array, with tombstones counted as occupied

examples.title: Double Hashing Implementation
examples.description: Java implementation of hash table with double hashing for collision resolution
examples.explanation: Uses two hash functions: h1 gives initial position, h2 determines step size. Probe sequence: (h1 + i×h2) mod m. This eliminates primary and secondary clustering. h2 must be non-zero and relatively prime to table size for full coverage.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) for table array, supports load factor up to ~0.7 before degradation
